text: In C++ when you pass an array to a function it decays into a pointer to its first element. Therefore sizeof(arr) inside the function is actually the size of an int* (a pointer) not the array. To fix this you must pass the size as a separate argument.

Because ptr is a pointer you cannot use the (.) operator directly. You have two options: The Arrow Operator: ptr->id (This is the standard and most readable way). Dereference First: (*ptr).id (You must use parentheses because (.) has higher precedence than *).

You are missing a base case. A recursive function needs a condition to stop calling itself. Without it factorial(5) calls factorial(4) then 3=>2=>1=>0=>-1=>-2... forever until the memory allocated for function calls (the stack) is full.

There are usually two reasons for this: Relative Path: If you are using an IDE like Visual Studio or CLion the working directory is often the build folder not your source code folder. Try using an absolute path (e.g. C:\\data.txt) to test if it works. File Extension: Ensure the file isn't actually named data.txt.txt (if Windows is hiding known extensions).
